did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning models, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) including VGG16 and MobileNetV2 combined with the Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM), to classify pigmented skin cancer images from the HAM10000 dataset. The study compares these AI architectures for the dermatology task of skin cancer image classification.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and comparison of three deep learning models (VGG16 with CBAM, MobileNetV2 with CBAM, and a hybrid VGG16-MobileNetV2) for skin cancer image classification. It details specific methodology including data preprocessing of the HAM10000 dataset, model training with the Adam optimizer, and reports original quantitative results such as validation accuracy (86% for MobileNetV2 with CBAM), precision, and recall. The abstract and introduction explicitly state the study's aim to compare these methods and evaluate their performance, which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: DEVELOPMENT OF SKIN CANCER PIGMENT IMAGE CLASSIFICATION USING A COMBINATION OF MOBILENETV2 AND CBAM
brief_summary: This study addresses the task of classifying pigmented skin cancer images by comparing three deep learning architectures: VGG16 with CBAM, MobileNetV2 with CBAM, and a hybrid VGG16-MobileNetV2 model. The researchers utilized the HAM10000 dataset from Kaggle, balancing it to 2,400 images across three classes: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Melanoma. The evaluation approach involved training the models using the Adam optimizer with a batch size of 16 and Early Stopping, assessing performance via validation accuracy, precision, and recall. The MobileNetV2 with CBAM model achieved the best performance with a validation accuracy of 86%, precision of 86.53%, and recall of 86.46%. In contrast, the VGG16 with CBAM model suffered from overfitting with only 54% accuracy, while the hybrid model reached 77% accuracy. The study concludes that combining MobileNetV2 with the Convolutional Block Attention Module provides superior stability and accuracy for skin cancer diagnosis compared to other tested architectures.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three primary classes used in the experiment after dataset balancing, with 800 images. The MobileNetV2 with CBAM model achieved 86% accuracy for this class.
One of the three primary classes used in the experiment after dataset balancing, with 800 images. The MobileNetV2 with CBAM model achieved 90% accuracy for this class.
One of the three primary classes used in the experiment after dataset balancing, with 800 images. The MobileNetV2 with CBAM model achieved 84% accuracy for this class.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of pigmented skin cancer (Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Melanoma)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'image classification' for 'pigmented skin cancer images'. The models predict one of three discrete classes (AKIEC, BCC, MEL) using a softmax activation layer in the output layer. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and confusion matrices, which are standard metrics for classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (Skin Cancer ISIC - The International Skin Imaging Collaboration)
datasets_used.description: A public dataset from Kaggle originally containing 10,015 images of seven types of skin cancer. For this study, it was balanced to include only three classes with 800 images each, totaling 2,400 images.

use.training: Used for training the VGG16 with CBAM, MobileNetV2 with CBAM, and hybrid VGG16-MobileNetV2 models. The dataset was split into 80% (1,920 samples) for training.
use.validation: Used for validation to monitor model performance and prevent overfitting. The dataset was split into 20% (480 samples) for validation.
use.other: The original dataset contained 10,015 images across seven classes, but only a balanced subset of 2,400 images across three classes was utilized for the experiments.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between the models were based on validation accuracy, precision, recall, and confusion matrices without statistical significance testing.

MobileNetV2 with CBAM.notes: Best performing model. Validation accuracy reached 86%. Highest misclassification rate was MEL class (12% misclassified as AKIEC).

VGG16-MobileNetV2 (Hybrid).notes: Validation accuracy fluctuated sharply in early epochs before stabilizing after epoch 30. Exhibited partial overfitting.

VGG16 with CBAM.notes: Experienced overfitting. Validation accuracy peaked at 55% before overfitting occurred. Training loss remained stable while validation loss rose significantly.

HAM10000 (Balanced Subset: 2400 images).notes: No separate test set was used; results are based on validation data. MobileNetV2 with CBAM demonstrated superior stability and accuracy compared to other methods.

architecture_used.main_model: Three architectures were compared: 1) VGG16 with Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM), 2) MobileNetV2 with CBAM, and 3) a hybrid VGG16-MobileNetV2 model. All utilized transfer learning.
architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning was used for the models (specifically VGG16 and MobileNetV2), implying pre-trained weights were utilized, though specific source datasets (e.g., ImageNet) were not explicitly named in the provided text.

architecture_details.mobile_net_features: Depth-wise separable convolutions and inverted residual blocks
architecture_details.vgg_features: Deep structure for strong feature extraction

training_details.early_stopping_note: Training stopped at different epochs depending on convergence; MobileNetV2 with CBAM stopped at epoch 59.
training_details.regularization: Dropout mentioned as a potential technique, but specific rates not reported for the final models.

preprocessing_techniques: Data balancing (reducing HAM10000 dataset to 2,400 images with 800 samples per class: Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, and Melanoma)

Data augmentation including rotation (±40°), horizontal and vertical shifts (0.2), scaling/shear (0.2), zooming (0.3), horizontal and vertical flipping, brightness adjustment (range [0.7, 1.3]), and channel shift (0.15)
Normalization via rescaling pixel values from 0–255 to 0–1 (rescale 1./255)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: width_shift_range of 0.2 (up to 20% of the image width)
height_shift_range of 0.2 (up to 20% of the image height)
zoom_range of 0.3 (up to 20% zoom in or out)
fill_mode set to 'nearest' for empty spaces after transformation

conclusions: The MobileNetV2 model combined with the Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM) achieved the best performance among the tested architectures, with a validation accuracy of 86%, precision of 86.53%, and recall of 86.46%.
The hybrid VGG16-MobileNetV2 model achieved a validation accuracy of 77%, performing better than VGG16 with CBAM but worse than MobileNetV2 with CBAM.
The VGG16 model with CBAM experienced significant overfitting, resulting in a low validation accuracy of 54% and poor generalization, particularly for the Basal Cell Carcinoma class (17% accuracy).
Combining MobileNetV2 with CBAM provides superior stability and accuracy for detecting pigmented skin cancer compared to previous single-model approaches or the hybrid VGG16-MobileNetV2 approach.
The developed system using MobileNetV2 with CBAM can serve as an effective tool for medical professionals to perform early and more accurate skin cancer diagnoses.

A key limitation of the study is the absence of a separate test set, meaning the reported performance on the validation set may be optimistically biased; future work should include a completely separate test set to validate generalization on unseen data.
important_remarks: The study lacks a separate test set, relying solely on a validation set (20% of data) for performance evaluation, which risks optimistic bias and limits the assessment of true generalization to unseen data.

The dataset was artificially balanced by reducing the HAM10000 dataset to 2,400 images (800 per class), discarding the majority of the original 10,015 images and ignoring four other skin cancer classes present in the source data.
The VGG16 with CBAM model suffered from significant overfitting, achieving only 54% validation accuracy, indicating poor generalization capability for this architecture on this specific task.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) was performed to validate the differences in performance between the compared models.

The study focuses only on three pigmented skin cancer classes (Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Melanoma), limiting its clinical relevance for broader dermatological diagnosis which includes non-pigmented or other lesion types.

The MobileNetV2 with CBAM model achieved a validation accuracy of 86%, but this metric is derived from a balanced subset rather than the full, imbalanced HAM10000 dataset, potentially inflating perceived performance for rare classes.
